articles.title: Why false sharing alignment should be 128 bytes on x64
articles.summary: False sharing can occur when two atomic variables reside on the same cache line, causing coordination between CPUs that doesn't happen per-address and leading to inefficient use of cache lines. To mitigate this issue, developers can separate atomics by making them reside in different cache lines using alignment directives such as Rust's #[repr(align(N))] or C11 _Alignas(N) or GCC/Clang __attribute__((aligned(N))). This simple solution can improve performance on x86_64 architectures and is recommended by experts.

articles.title: Eliminating Go bound checks with unsafe

By removing these checks, developers can optimize their code for better performance, particularly on systems with limited cache capacity or branch prediction issues.

articles.title: Radicle: P2P Git Replication with Git Native Issues and Patches
articles.summary: Radicle is an open source, peer-to-peer code collaboration stack built on Git that enables users to run their own nodes and fosters a resilient network without reliance on third-parties. The Radicle protocol leverages cryptographic identities for code and social artifacts, utilizes Git for efficient data transfer between peers, and employs a custom gossip protocol for exchanging repository metadata. This allows developers to extend Radicle's capabilities to build any kind of collaboration flow they see fit, making it an evolvable and extensible platform.

articles.title: OpenSSH 10.4

Additionally, it adds documentation notes for certain security-related options and disables a feature that was previously documented to be ineffective.
